_about: planwright worker-settings profile (REQ-F1.8, D-38). A Claude Code settings fragment that a /orchestrate worker (background subagent or headless tmux/cron worker) runs under so the ROUTINE /execute-task toolset is pre-approved and the autonomy loop does not stall on permission prompts. planwright never edits settings.json itself (REQ-I1.2): merge this into the worker's settings (or reference it) the same way hook wiring is a manual step. AUTO-APPROVE HOOK: the hooks.PreToolUse (Bash) entry below wires scripts/worker-command-guard.sh, the deterministic worker command-guard from worker-permission-ergonomics (REQ-C1.1). It reads the Claude Code PreToolUse payload on stdin and extracts the fully-expanded Bash command Claude Code hands it (variables already expanded before the hook runs), then returns permissionDecision: allow ONLY for an enumerated set of known-safe command shapes (read-only coreutils / git / gh, repo scripts and tests, mise / bats, and control structures whose bodies are themselves verified), deferring everything else to the normal permission flow — so the routine /execute-task toolset stops flooding on the expanded shapes Claude Code cannot statically analyze ($VAR-path script calls, for/while loops). Its properties: no-LLM (pure-shell analysis, no model in the decision path, honoring fleet-autonomy D-18); allow-only (it can ONLY auto-approve — it never emits deny or ask, so it is never worse than today); deny-precedence (a hook allow can never un-block a command the deny block below forbids, because Claude Code evaluates deny before ask before allow regardless of hook output). It references the script through ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT} exactly as the plugin's hooks/hooks.json does, so it resolves under a marketplace install, and it is deliberately NOT registered in that plugin-global hooks/hooks.json (REQ-C1.2). WORKER-SCOPED — DO NOT MIS-MERGE: this auto-approve hook stanza belongs ONLY in a dispatched worker's settings (this worker-settings.json), never in a tower or human interactive settings.json, because worker-scoping is enforced only by where the hook is wired, not by any runtime session-type check (REQ-C1.2, R7); merging it into a general ~/.claude/settings.json would silently auto-approve those known-safe shapes in your own interactive and tower sessions — the blast radius D-5 rejects. OPTIONAL literal-path allow entry: as defense-in-depth for the hook's jq-absent degradation path, an adopter MAY additionally add a persistent Bash allow entry matching the resolved literal absolute path of the plugin scripts their workers invoke (for example `Bash(/abs/path/to/planwright/scripts/*)`); this path is install-location-specific, so it stays adopter-documented rather than shipped (D-7, REQ-D1.1). SECURITY: this is a permissions allowlist and needs human sign-off before use; the deny block encodes planwright's hard invariants (never merge / force-push / amend / squash / rebase) as guardrails, but review the allow list against your repo's risk posture first. `gh pr ready` is allowed (not denied) so /spec-kickoff can mark the spec PR ready as its terminal step — the narrow exception to bootstrap D-26's all-drafts rule that kickoff-lifecycle D-6 carries (only the spec PR, by /spec-kickoff; task PRs stay drafts; merge stays human, never auto-merge); the permission un-blocks the command, while the only-the-spec-PR scope is enforced by the skill, not the glob. The `gh pr ready --undo` form (both `gh pr ready --undo` and the `gh pr ready <pr> --undo` flag-after-arg spelling) is explicitly denied so a worker can only un-draft a PR, never re-draft one. Deny precedence: Claude Code evaluates deny before allow, so the deny rules below override the broad `git push origin` allow regardless of specificity. The push denies close the known evasions of the force/main guardrails, not just the flag-immediately-after-`git push` form: `git push * --force*` / `git push * -f*` catch force flags placed AFTER the remote (e.g. `git push origin --force`); `git push *+*` catches the `+<refspec>` force form (e.g. `git push origin +HEAD:main`); `git push *:main` / `git push * main` catch the short (`HEAD:main`) and bare (`origin main`) `main` destinations, and `git push *refs/heads/main` / `git push *heads/main` catch the fully-qualified and abbreviated `refs/heads/main` spellings; `--mirror`/`--all` are denied as bulk-ref escape hatches. These are glob deny rules matched as literal substrings, so they are best-effort against exotic refspec spellings rather than exhaustive: review them against your repo's risk posture before relying on the fragment. Compound commands are parsed per-subcommand by Claude Code, so a denied push cannot be smuggled via `&&`/`;`/`|`/subshell.
